solution_verified:
## Claim

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Let \({r\brack s}_q\) denote the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with value \(0\) if \(s<0\), \(r<0\), or \(s>r\). Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients for \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

## Proof

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(q\) records bosonic degree, \(u\) records fermionic degree, and \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive super-Schur function. This is the Hilbert-series specialization of the universal super-Schur character expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Second, the superspace Frobenius formula gives, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]
This is Theorem 5.2 together with the Schur expansion in equation (2.5) of the Fields-conjecture formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))

We first record the needed one-variable super-Schur specialization.

### Lemma

In one even Hilbert variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

### Proof of the lemma

Use the positive hook-Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\mu(q)=q^r\). Also, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, i.e. has at most one box in each column; in that case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so \(\lambda'\) has at least two boxes in its second column. Removing \(\mu'=(1^r)\) removes boxes only from the first column, so \(\lambda'/\mu'\) still has two boxes in the second column. Thus no term contributes.

If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only choices of \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). These give respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

Now expand the superspace Hilbert series using the lemma:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)
+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The \((0,0)\)-bidegree of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, so
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\). Pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\) forgets the \(S_n\)-module structure, since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
By RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, and \(\operatorname{Des}(Q)=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q . \tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]
We show \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Thus
\[
D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1.
\]
By (1), \(C_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the contribution of \(w\) to \(H_b(q)\) in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q).
\]
Together with (1) and the already proved equality \(C_0=D_0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally, use the standard rectangle interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

solution_verified:
## Claim

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Let \({r\brack s}_q\) denote the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with value \(0\) if \(s<0\), \(r<0\), or \(s>r\). For a rectangle \(r\times s\), write \(\rho\subseteq r\times s\) to mean that the Ferrers diagram of \(\rho\) has at most \(r\) rows and at most \(s\) columns. Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

## Proof

We use two standard inputs. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant algebra, i.e. the quotient of
\[
\mathbb{Q}[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)
\]
by the ideal generated by positive-degree diagonal \(S_n\)-invariants. This is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\) in the Fields-conjecture formula.

First, Lentfer’s diagonal-supersymmetry result, Proposition 5.1 of Lentfer, arXiv:2505.14885, gives the Hilbert-series super-Schur expansion with the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\):
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(q\) records bosonic degree, \(u\) records fermionic degree, and \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive super-Schur function in one even and one odd variable.

Second, with the above identification \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), Theorem 5.2 together with the Schur expansion in equation (2.5) of the Fields-conjecture formula, arXiv:2505.24027, gives, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]

We first record the needed one-variable super-Schur specialization.

### Lemma

In one even Hilbert variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
If \(\lambda\neq\varnothing\) is not a hook, then
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

### Proof of the lemma

Use the positive hook-Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\mu(q)=q^r\). Also, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, i.e. has at most one box in each column; in that case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

If \(\lambda\neq\varnothing\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so \(\lambda'\) has at least two boxes in its second column. Removing \(\mu'=(1^r)\) removes boxes only from the first column, so \(\lambda'/\mu'\) still has two boxes in the second column. Thus no term contributes.

If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only choices of \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). These give respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

Now expand the superspace Hilbert series using the lemma:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)
+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The \((0,0)\)-bidegree of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, represented by constants, so
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\). Pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\) forgets the \(S_n\)-module structure:
\[
H_b(q)
=
\left\langle [u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),\,h_1^n\right\rangle .
\]
Since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\qquad
f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
By the standard RSK descent correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, with
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q)=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q . \tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]
We show \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Thus
\[
D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1.
\]
By (1), \(C_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Using the zero convention for Gaussian binomial coefficients, the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the contribution of \(w\) to \(H_b(q)\) in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q).
\]
Together with (1) and the already proved equality \(C_0=D_0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally, use the standard rectangle interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Identification of coefficients.** Let \(\tilde c_\lambda(n)\) be Lentfer’s coefficients. Taking \((k,j)=(m,0)\) gives \(R_n^{(m,0)}=R_n^{(m)}\), and the defining formula for positive super-Schur functions gives \(s_\lambda(q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(q)\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=\sum_{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le \min(m,n)}\tilde c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m).
\]
By uniqueness of the Schur expansion in \(m\) variables, these \(\tilde c_\lambda(n)\) are the original universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), and all coefficients with \(\ell(\lambda)>n\) are zero.

2. **Fields-index substitution.** In Theorem 5.2, the coefficient of fermionic degree \(b\) is obtained by setting \(k=n-b\). For \(0\le b\le n-1\), equation (2.5) gives
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]
For \(b\ge n\), both sides are zero because \(SR_n\) has no such fermionic degree in the Fields formula and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\le n-1\).

3. **Frobenius dimension identity.** If \(\operatorname{grFrob}(V;q)=\sum_{\mu} a_\mu(q)s_\mu\), then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(V;q)=\sum_\mu a_\mu(q)\dim S^\mu
=\sum_\mu a_\mu(q)f^\mu
=\left\langle \operatorname{grFrob}(V;q),h_1^n\right\rangle,
\]
since \(h_1^n=p_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu\). This justifies the passage from Frobenius series to Hilbert series degreewise.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

2. **Fields-index substitution.** In Theorem 5.2, the coefficient of fermionic degree \(b\) is obtained by setting \(k=n-b\). For \(0\le b\le n-1\), equation (2.5) gives
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]
For \(b\ge n\), both sides are zero because \(SR_n\) has no such fermionic degree in the Fields formula and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\le n-1\).

3. **Frobenius dimension identity.** If \(\operatorname{grFrob}(V;q)=\sum_{\mu} a_\mu(q)s_\mu\), then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(V;q)=\sum_\mu a_\mu(q)\dim S^\mu
=\sum_\mu a_\mu(q)f^\mu
=\left\langle \operatorname{grFrob}(V;q),h_1^n\right\rangle,
\]
since \(h_1^n=p_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu\). This justifies the passage from Frobenius series to Hilbert series degreewise.

=
\#\{(w,\rho): d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ \rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently, it establishes the stated generating-function identity with \({d-1\brack b}_q\).

$$

c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix an integer \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables, and let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)=(a,\underbrace{1,\ldots,1}_{b\text{ times}})
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). The empty partition is denoted \(\varnothing\).

For integers \(r,s\), use the Gaussian binomial convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i}
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq s\times t\) means that \(\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_s)\) is a partition with
\[
t\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge \rho_s\ge 0,
\]
and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_s\). For \(s=0\), there is one empty partition of size \(0\).

The result proved is:

\[
\boxed{c_\varnothing(n)=1.}
\]

For every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[2mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation; the statement also records the degree-zero coefficient \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

## Proof

We use three standard Hilbert-series inputs.

1. **Super-Schur universality.** The same Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-bosonic, one-fermionic superspace specialization:
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
   \]
   where \(q\) records the ordinary bosonic degree and \(u\) is an ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable.

2. **One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)
   =
   q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook shapes vanish in this specialization.

3. **Superspace Hilbert formula.** The Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Hilbert formula, equivalently after RSK, gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
   \]

First, the degree-zero component of the coinvariant algebra is one-dimensional, and only \(s_\varnothing\) contributes to total degree zero. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set \(C_{-1}(q)=0\). Also define
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the super-Schur specialization,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from the product formula. Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
this equals
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using the standard subset form of the Gaussian binomial,
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We show that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\).

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and again write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge 1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the \(w\)-summand in formula \((2)\).

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\). There is exactly one permutation with \(d=0\), namely the identity permutation, and its contribution to \(H_0(q)\) is \(1\). This is exactly supplied by the term \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\).

Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Using
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
we get
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed combinatorial interpretation.

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula. The empty coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). When \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\), and \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible and the coefficient vanishes. For \(n=1\), the only permutation has no descents, so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.
verifier_response:
<CITATION_AUDIT>
- [Result: Super-Schur universality / diagonal supersymmetry] VERIFIED AS A SUBSTITUTE, BUT NOT CITED IN THE PROOF — Lentfer’s preprint “Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings” states that bosonic–fermionic coinvariant-ring character series expand in super Schur functions \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) with universal coefficients independent of \((k,j)\), and that for the symmetric group this proves Bergeron’s diagonal supersymmetry conjecture. This supports the invoked result, but the solution itself gives no citation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))
- [Paper: Rhoades–Wilson, “The Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring”] NOT VERIFIED AS STATED — The paper exists and Theorem 5.3 gives \({\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\). The setup matches the one-bosonic/one-fermionic superspace coinvariant ring. However, the exact permutation product formula involving \({\rm maj}\), \({\rm des}\), and \(\prod_{i=1}^{\rm des(w)}(q^i+u)\) was not found in the searchable paper text; an additional Carlitz/q-Stirling permutation identity is needed. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8/core-reader))
- [Named standard theorem: RSK] VERIFIED AS STANDARD, BUT INSUFFICIENTLY USED — RSK is standardly a bijection between generalized permutations/biwords and pairs of semistandard tableaux of common shape. The proof’s phrase “equivalently after RSK” does not by itself justify the displayed \({\rm maj}/{\rm des}\) product formula. ([doc.sagemath.org](https://doc.sagemath.org/html/en/reference/combinat/sage/combinat/rsk.html))
- [Named standard identity: \(q\)-Pascal identity] VERIFIED — The Gaussian binomial identity used in the proof,
  \[
  \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
  =
  \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
  +
  q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
  \]
  matches the standard Pascal analogue. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_binomial_coefficient))
- [Standard Gaussian-binomial rectangle interpretation] VERIFIED — The use of \(\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q\) as the generating function for partitions fitting in an \(s\times(r-s)\) rectangle is standard. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integer_partition))
- [Standard super/hook Schur specialization] VERIFIED/DERIVABLE — Standard references define hook/supersymmetric Schur functions and give the skew-Schur expansion \(s_\lambda(\mathbf x/\mathbf y)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf x)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf y)\), from which the one-even/one-odd hook formula follows. ([symmetricfunctions.com](https://www.symmetricfunctions.com/superSymmetricSchur.htm))
</CITATION_AUDIT>

<MAJOR_GAPS>
- (none)
</MAJOR_GAPS>

<MINOR_GAPS>
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The proof invokes the central “Super-Schur universality” theorem without a precise citation or hypothesis check. This is fixable by citing the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings and explaining that the Hilbert coefficients obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-character are the same coefficients as in the purely bosonic expansion.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The superspace Hilbert product formula is attributed to “Fields/Rhoades-Wilson” but Rhoades–Wilson state the Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form, not in the displayed \({\rm maj}/{\rm des}\) product form. The proof needs an inserted Carlitz/q-Stirling identity, or a direct citation to the product form.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The one-even/one-odd super-Schur specialization is stated without proof. It is a short standard derivation, but for strict rigor it should be included or cited.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is not explicitly defined in the proof before use. It should be identified with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)/SI_n\).
</MINOR_GAPS>

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for super-Schur universality.** Insert the following black-box theorem with citation:

   For fixed \(n\), let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) be the diagonal bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. The diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
   \]
   with coefficients independent of \(k,j\). Taking \(j=0\) gives the original bosonic expansion
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};\mathbf q)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
   \]
   Taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used in the proof. If the cited theorem is stated at the Frobenius-character level, first forget the \(S_n\)-character by replacing each irreducible character by its dimension; the same universal coefficients then give the Hilbert-series expansion.

2. **Patch for the superspace Hilbert product formula.** Replace the unsupported sentence by:

   Rhoades–Wilson prove
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,k).
   \]
   By the Carlitz identity
   \[
   \sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u),
   \]
   this is exactly the product formula used.

   A concise proof of the Carlitz identity can be added as follows. Let
   \[
   E_{n,d}(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)=d}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
   \]
   The standard insertion-of-\(n\) argument gives the \(q\)-Eulerian recurrence
   \[
   E_{n,d}=[d+1]_qE_{n-1,d}+q^d[n-d]_qE_{n-1,d-1}.
   \]
   The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the product side is
   \[
   A_{n,b}:=\sum_d q^{-bd+\binom b2}\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_qE_{n,d}(q).
   \]
   Substituting the recurrence for \(E_{n,d}\) and using \(q\)-Pascal plus
   \[
   [r+1]_q\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   [b]_q\begin{bmatrix}r+1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   \]
   gives
   \[
   A_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(A_{n-1,b-1}+A_{n-1,b}).
   \]
   The coefficient
   \[
   G_{n,b}:=[n-b]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
   \]
   in the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence by the defining recurrence for \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\), with the same initial values. Hence \(A_{n,b}=G_{n,b}\) for all \(b\).

3. **Patch for the super-Schur specialization.** Add the derivation:
   Using
   \[
   s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
   \]
   \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a one-row partition, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip. Thus nonempty non-hooks vanish for one even and one odd variable. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving \(\nu\)’s are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The proof invokes the central “Super-Schur universality” theorem without a precise citation or hypothesis check. This is fixable by citing the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings and explaining that the Hilbert coefficients obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-character are the same coefficients as in the purely bosonic expansion.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The superspace Hilbert product formula is attributed to “Fields/Rhoades-Wilson” but Rhoades–Wilson state the Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form, not in the displayed \({\rm maj}/{\rm des}\) product form. The proof needs an inserted Carlitz/q-Stirling identity, or a direct citation to the product form.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The one-even/one-odd super-Schur specialization is stated without proof. It is a short standard derivation, but for strict rigor it should be included or cited.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is not explicitly defined in the proof before use. It should be identified with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)/SI_n\).

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for super-Schur universality.** Insert the following black-box theorem with citation:

   For fixed \(n\), let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) be the diagonal bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. The diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
   \]
   with coefficients independent of \(k,j\). Taking \(j=0\) gives the original bosonic expansion
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};\mathbf q)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
   \]
   Taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used in the proof. If the cited theorem is stated at the Frobenius-character level, first forget the \(S_n\)-character by replacing each irreducible character by its dimension; the same universal coefficients then give the Hilbert-series expansion.

2. **Patch for the superspace Hilbert product formula.** Replace the unsupported sentence by:

   Rhoades–Wilson prove
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,k).
   \]
   By the Carlitz identity
   \[
   \sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u),
   \]
   this is exactly the product formula used.

   A concise proof of the Carlitz identity can be added as follows. Let
   \[
   E_{n,d}(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)=d}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
   \]
   The standard insertion-of-\(n\) argument gives the \(q\)-Eulerian recurrence
   \[
   E_{n,d}=[d+1]_qE_{n-1,d}+q^d[n-d]_qE_{n-1,d-1}.
   \]
   The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the product side is
   \[
   A_{n,b}:=\sum_d q^{-bd+\binom b2}\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_qE_{n,d}(q).
   \]
   Substituting the recurrence for \(E_{n,d}\) and using \(q\)-Pascal plus
   \[
   [r+1]_q\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   [b]_q\begin{bmatrix}r+1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   \]
   gives
   \[
   A_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(A_{n-1,b-1}+A_{n-1,b}).
   \]
   The coefficient
   \[
   G_{n,b}:=[n-b]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
   \]
   in the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence by the defining recurrence for \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\), with the same initial values. Hence \(A_{n,b}=G_{n,b}\) for all \(b\).

3. **Patch for the super-Schur specialization.** Add the derivation:
   Using
   \[
   s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
   \]
   \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a one-row partition, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip. Thus nonempty non-hooks vanish for one even and one odd variable. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving \(\nu\)’s are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

solution_verified:
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\) in \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables, and let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be its universal Schur-Hilbert expansion. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[1mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[1mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \({\rm des}(w)\) is the number of descents of \(w\), \({\rm maj}(w)\) is the sum of its descent positions, and \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings: for fixed \(n\), the Hilbert series of the \((k,j)\)-bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring has an expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same Hilbert-level coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) for all \(k,j\). If the theorem is stated at the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-character level, evaluating the character at the identity gives this Hilbert-series form. Taking \(j=0\) gives the purely bosonic expansion above, and taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Let
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)/SI_n
\]
be the superspace coinvariant ring, where \(SI_n\) is generated by \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants with zero constant term. Rhoades–Wilson prove its bigraded Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form; equivalently, by the Carlitz \(q\)-Eulerian/\(q\)-Stirling identity,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{1}
\]
The quotient definition and the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula are those of Rhoades–Wilson. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763))

Now compute the one-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization. With the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, every nonempty non-hook shape vanishes. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (2) in the superspace expansion,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(b\ge 0\),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from (1). Fix \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand of (1) is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge 1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (4). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge 0.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook interpretation.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for the Carlitz normalization used in (1).** Insert the following lemma after the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula.

Let \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) be defined by
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)={\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q{\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k),
\qquad {\rm Stir}_q(0,k)=\delta_{0,k}.
\]
For \(0\le b\le n\), set
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
\]
and
\[
E_{n,b}=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q .
\]
Then \(O_{n,b}=E_{n,b}\).

Proof: From the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence,
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(O_{n-1,b}+O_{n-1,b-1}\bigr),
\]
with the usual convention that out-of-range terms are zero.

Now prove the same recurrence for \(E_{n,b}\) by inserting \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after a descent of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, keep the descent number \(e\); their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{{e}\brack b}_q [e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(n-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,n-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}\,
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n,b}\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket equals
\[
[n-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{{e+1}\brack b}_q={{e}\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{{e}\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution equals \([n-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}\). Hence
\[
E_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The initial conditions agree, so \(E_{n,b}=O_{n,b}\).

Therefore the Rhoades–Wilson formula
\[
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
has \(u^b\)-coefficient \(E_{n,b}\), which is exactly the \(u^b\)-coefficient of
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This proves formula (1).

2. **Patch for hooks of length \(>n\).** Add: By diagonal supersymmetry / Howe-duality support, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur in the universal Hilbert expansion, so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n\). On the claimed combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible for \(b\ge n-1\) when appropriate. Thus the asserted formula remains valid in the out-of-support hook cases.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n,b}\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket equals
\[
[n-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{{e+1}\brack b}_q={{e}\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{{e}\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution equals \([n-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}\). Hence
\[
E_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The initial conditions agree, so \(E_{n,b}=O_{n,b}\).

Therefore the Rhoades–Wilson formula
\[
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
has \(u^b\)-coefficient \(E_{n,b}\), which is exactly the \(u^b\)-coefficient of
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This proves formula (1).

2. **Patch for hooks of length \(>n\).** Add: By diagonal supersymmetry / Howe-duality support, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur in the universal Hilbert expansion, so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n\). On the claimed combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible for \(b\ge n-1\) when appropriate. Thus the asserted formula remains valid in the out-of-support hook cases.
parsed.problem_solved: With the two routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed hook formula for the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(S_n\): \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is counted by the stated pairs \((w,\rho)\). The proof relies on the verified diagonal supersymmetry theorem and the verified Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert series theorem, plus the patched Carlitz coefficient identity.

c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
Insert the following black-box setup before the proof’s Step 1.

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. By diagonal supersymmetry, for fixed \(n\) there are nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu.
\]
For the original purely bosonic ring \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)\). Forgetting the \(S_n\)-structure sends the irreducible indexed by \(\mu\) to dimension \(f^\mu\), so
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
\]
For \(k=j=1\), \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\). Also \(c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}\), since the total degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation. This supplies the missing bridge between the original Hilbert coefficients and the \(SR_n\) calculation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Next cite the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization:
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and non-hook shapes vanish under this specialization. This is also directly derivable from
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally replace “Fields’ formula gives” by the theorem of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{b\ge0}u^b
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is equivalent to the product form used in the proof because, for \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2.
\]
After these insertions, the remaining recurrence, \(q\)-Pascal simplification, Gaussian-binomial rectangle interpretation, and RSK translation are valid as written. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. By diagonal supersymmetry, for fixed \(n\) there are nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu.
\]
For the original purely bosonic ring \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)\). Forgetting the \(S_n\)-structure sends the irreducible indexed by \(\mu\) to dimension \(f^\mu\), so
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
\]
For \(k=j=1\), \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\). Also \(c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}\), since the total degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation. Next cite the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization:
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and non-hook shapes vanish under this specialization. This is also directly derivable from
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]

Finally replace “Fields’ formula gives” by the theorem of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{b\ge0}u^b
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is equivalent to the product form used in the proof because, for \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2.
\]

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.
claim_verified: With the two routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed hook formula for the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(S_n\): \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is counted by the stated pairs \((w,\rho)\). The proof relies on the verified diagonal supersymmetry theorem and the verified Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert series theorem, plus the patched Carlitz coefficient identity.
solution_verified:
**Claim.** For fixed \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be the universal Schur expansion for the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\). Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry gives, for the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). We use the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables, so
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
Also, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur.

Second, for the one-boson, one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\), and
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(N,K)
=
{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K-1)+[K]_q{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K),
\qquad
{\rm Stir}_q(0,K)=\delta_{0,K}.
\]

We first record the required Carlitz normalization. For \(0\le b\le N\), set
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(N,N-b),
\]
and
\[
E_{N,b}
=
\sum_{w\in \mathfrak S_N}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Indeed, the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q\bigl(O_{N-1,b}+O_{N-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The same recurrence holds for \(E_{N,b}\). Insert \(N\) into a permutation \(v\in\mathfrak S_{N-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\), \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after descents of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, preserve the descent number; their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{e\brack b}_q[e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(N-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,N-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of all insertions of \(N\) into \(v\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket becomes
\[
[N-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{e+1\brack b}_q={e\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{e\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is \([N-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{N-1,b}+E_{N-1,b-1}\). The initial conditions agree, hence \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Therefore, if
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u),
\]
then
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{1}
\]

Now compute the same coefficient using supersymmetry. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, the only nonempty \(\lambda\) that survive are hooks. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
The empty partition contributes \(1\), so by (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed nonidentity \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so this equals the \(w\)-summand in (1). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, whose contribution to (1) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all } b\ge0.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus (5) becomes
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed counting formula.

The constant term of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants, and no nonempty Schur polynomial has degree \(0\), so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Also, if \(b\ge n\), then \(\ell(a,1^b)>n\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\); on the combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the required condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus the formula holds in all hook cases.

solution_verified:
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\). Also
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\qquad
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\quad\text{if }b\ge n-1.
\]

We prove this using the refined Frobenius coefficients from diagonal supersymmetry, the one-bosonic-one-fermionic super Schur specialization, and the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem on the superspace coinvariant ring.

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Thus \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), and \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\). Diagonal supersymmetry gives nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu,
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). For the purely bosonic ring, \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure gives
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu},
\qquad f^\mu:=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|.
\]
The degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation, so
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
These are the standard diagonal-supersymmetry inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and every non-hook shape specializes to \(0\). This follows from the super Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally, the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}u^r
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-r\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is their \(C_{n,k}\)-form with \(r=n-k\). Equivalently, it matches the product form
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
\]
because
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-r+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-r+1}{2}
=
-rd+\binom r2.
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

We use the convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

For fixed \(\mu\vdash n\), let
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q):=[u^b s_\mu]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
From Murai–Rhoades–Wilson,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q):=0.
\]
Using the one-variable super Schur specialization,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

We now solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The unique standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]

Assume \(b\ge1\). For \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Using the recurrence and the induction hypothesis,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
and the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\quad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive under \(d\ge b+1\), since the sum of \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

Now pass from refined Frobenius coefficients to Hilbert coefficients:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing a second standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples \((\mu,T,U,\rho)\) with \(T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) satisfying the conditions above.

By the Robinson–Schensted correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Taking \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\), the quadruples are therefore in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d\ge b+1\ge n\) is impossible. Hence the counting set is empty, and
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad(b\ge n-1).
\]
