solution_verified:
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in\mathfrak S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad 
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and, for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in\mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\[2pt]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\[2pt]
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le b\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

---

### Proof

We use the diagonal superspace coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), with \(k\) commuting variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting variable sets. Its graded Frobenius series has the universal expansion
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,
s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function, \(\mu\vdash n\), and the coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\) are independent of \(k,j\).

Applying the dimension map on Frobenius characteristics,
\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
turns graded Frobenius series into ordinary Hilbert series. Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the coefficients in the original purely bosonic Hilbert series.

The constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)})\) is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive degree. Therefore
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now specialize to one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\). The positive super Schur function satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), and a one-variable skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero exactly when \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip, only hooks survive. More precisely,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hooks vanish under this specialization.

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
and define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
From the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) directly. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i: i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\},
\]
and define \(d(T)\) and \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) analogously.

The superspace coinvariant Frobenius formula gives
\[
[u^r]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom r2-rd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Equivalently, since
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}-rd+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we may write
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)\,
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]

Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu\) gives the Hilbert series. By RSK, permutations \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\operatorname{Des}(Q(w)).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) yields
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Indeed, the identity permutation has \(d=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes only \(\delta_{b,0}\) to \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\). For a nonidentity permutation \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (2). Hence (3) holds.

Comparing (1) and (3), and using \(C_{-1}^{(n)}=D_{-1}^{(n)}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the claimed hook-coefficient interpretation.

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

solution_verified:
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in\mathfrak S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad 
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and, for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le b\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** Work over a characteristic-zero field. We use the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), with \(k\) commuting sets and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables, with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. The diagonal supersymmetry theorem says that, for finite groups in characteristic \(0\), the graded Frobenius series has a universal positive-super-Schur expansion
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,
s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\). Since \(\mathfrak S_n\) is finite, this theorem applies here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Applying the dimension map on Frobenius characteristics,
\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu,
\]
turns graded Frobenius series into Hilbert series. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda} c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the universal coefficients in the original purely bosonic Hilbert-Schur expansion.

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, hence has dimension \(1\), while every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Therefore
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now specialize to one bosonic grading variable \(q\) and one fermionic grading variable \(u\). The positive super Schur function satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a single row, and a one-variable skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\) is nonzero exactly when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, only hooks survive. More precisely,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, for a hook \((a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\).

Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
and
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
The preceding specialization gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) directly. Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The proved Fields Conjecture for the superspace coinvariant ring gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{\,n-k}C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q),
\]
where
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)d(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Here \(\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i:i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\}\), \(d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|\), and \(\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i\). The theorem applies because \(SR_n\) is exactly the rank-\(n\) superspace coinvariant ring with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))

Setting \(r=n-k\), we get
\[
[u^r]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom r2-rd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Applying the dimension map gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom b2-bd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Under RSK, \(w\leftrightarrow(P(w),Q(w))\), one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\operatorname{Des}(Q(w)),
\]
and for each fixed recording tableau \(Q\) of shape \(\mu\), there are \(f^\mu\) possible insertion tableaux \(P\). Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
The identity permutation has \(d=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes only \(\delta_{b,0}\) to (2). For any nonidentity \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (2). Hence (3) holds.

Comparing (1) and (3), and using \(C_{-1}^{(n)}=D_{-1}^{(n)}=0\), we get by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the claimed hook-coefficient interpretation.

c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=

solution_verified:
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For \(0\le b\le r\), use
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r-b\). Also set \({r\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>r\).

Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficient of the empty partition is
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\,
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

**Proof.** Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. Diagonal supersymmetry gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking ordinary Hilbert series means taking dimensions of the \(S_n\)-modules, so \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\) is replaced by \(f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\). Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n}\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the coefficient in parentheses is exactly the bosonic coefficient \(c_\lambda(n)\). Therefore the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurs for the one-even, one-odd specialization \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

We next evaluate \(s_\lambda(q/u)\). By the hook-Schur identity,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\). Also \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

Thus
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1.
\]
If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\), and the only contributing \(\nu=(r)\) are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). They give
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
If \(\lambda\) is nonempty and not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so for every \(r\), the skew shape \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) has at least two cells in some column; hence it is not a horizontal strip. Therefore \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) for nonhook \(\lambda\).

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Now we compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. The superspace Frobenius formula of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k}.
\]
The Wilson/Haglund–Rhoades–Shimozono Schur expansion says that for \(b=n-k\),
\[
C_{n,n-b}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{\lambda\vdash n}\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\lambda)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q\,s_\lambda(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking dimensions replaces \(s_\lambda(\mathbf z)\) by \(f^\lambda=\# {\rm SYT}(\lambda)\). Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, with
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\); this accounts for the extra \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus (5) holds.

Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
A_b(q)=B_b(q)\qquad(b\ge0).
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into the last formula gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.
claim_verified:
Over characteristic \(0\), the proof establishes the claimed hook interpretation: \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\{(w,\rho): d(w)\ge b+1,\ \rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\tbinom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
solution_verified:
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in\mathfrak S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad 
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and, for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le b\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** Work over a characteristic-zero field. We use the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), with \(k\) commuting sets and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables, with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. By Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem for coinvariant rings, specifically Theorem 1.2 and Corollary 1.3 of *Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings* together with its Hilbert-series form in Proposition 5.1, the graded Frobenius series has a universal positive-super-Schur expansion
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,
s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\).

Applying the dimension map on Frobenius characteristics,
\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu,
\]
turns graded Frobenius series into Hilbert series. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda} c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu .
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the universal coefficients in the original purely bosonic Hilbert-Schur expansion.

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, hence has dimension \(1\), while every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Therefore
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now specialize to one bosonic grading variable \(q\) and one fermionic grading variable \(u\). The standard positive super Schur identity is
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a single row, and a one-variable skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\) is nonzero exactly when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, only hooks survive. More precisely,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, for a hook \((a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\).

Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
and
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
The preceding specialization gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) directly. Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The proved Fields Conjecture for the superspace coinvariant ring, namely Murai–Rhoades–Wilson, *A proof of the Fields Conjectures*, Theorem 5.2, together with their Schur expansion of \(C_{n,k}\) in equation (2.5), gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{\,n-k}C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q),
\]
where
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)d(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Here \(\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i:i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\}\), \(d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|\), and \(\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i\).

Setting \(r=n-k\), we get
\[
[u^r]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom r2-rd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Applying the dimension map gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom b2-bd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Under RSK, \(w\leftrightarrow(P(w),Q(w))\), the standard descent property gives
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\operatorname{Des}(Q(w)),
\]
and for each fixed recording tableau \(Q\) of shape \(\mu\), there are \(f^\mu\) possible insertion tableaux \(P\). See, for example, Sagan, *The Symmetric Group*, §3.3, or Stanley, *Enumerative Combinatorics*, Vol. 2, §7.11. Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
The identity permutation has \(d=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes only \(\delta_{b,0}\) to (2). For any nonidentity \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the standard \(q\)-Pascal identity for Gaussian coefficients,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
with the usual convention that the coefficient is \(0\) outside its range, this equals the \(w\)-summand in (2). Hence (3) holds. The \(q\)-Pascal identity and the partition interpretation of Gaussian coefficients used here are standard; see Stanley, *Enumerative Combinatorics*, Vol. 1, §1.7, especially Proposition 1.7.3.

Comparing (1) and (3), and using \(C_{-1}^{(n)}=D_{-1}^{(n)}=0\), we get by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Finally, by the Gaussian coefficient interpretation as partitions in a rectangle,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the claimed hook-coefficient interpretation.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
Insert the following base-change lemma near the beginning.

**Base-change lemma.** Let \(K\) be a characteristic-zero field, and let \(A_K\) be the bosonic-fermionic polynomial/exterior algebra with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. For any field extension \(L/K\),
\[
(A_K^{\mathfrak S_n})\otimes_K L \cong (A_K\otimes_K L)^{\mathfrak S_n},
\]
because \(|\mathfrak S_n|=n!\) is invertible in \(K\), so the Reynolds idempotent
\[
\frac1{n!}\sum_{\sigma\in\mathfrak S_n}\sigma
\]
splits invariants degreewise and commutes with scalar extension. Hence the ideal generated by positive-degree invariants also commutes with scalar extension:
\[
(A_K^{\mathfrak S_n})_+A_K\otimes_K L
=
(A_L^{\mathfrak S_n})_+A_L.
\]
Therefore
\[
R_{n,K}^{(k,j)}\otimes_K L \cong R_{n,L}^{(k,j)}
\]
as multigraded vector spaces, and the multigraded Hilbert series is independent of the chosen characteristic-zero field. Since the whole construction is defined over \(\mathbb Q\), Lentfer’s \(\mathbb C\)-result determines the same Hilbert-Schur coefficients over any characteristic-zero field.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.
claim_verified:
After inserting the minor citation, hypothesis, and range checks above, the proof establishes the claimed hook interpretation: \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
\]
solution_verified:
**Claim.** For \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\quad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i .
\]
Let \({r\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>r\), and for \(0\le b\le r\),
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times c\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(c\). Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\tbinom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
\]

**Proof.** Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(k,j\ge0\), let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) be the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables, with \(S_n\) acting by permutation matrices on the index \(1,\dots,n\). Thus \(R_n^{(k,0)}\) is the usual bosonic diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) variable sets, and \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring.

By diagonal supersymmetry, applied to the finite group \(S_n\subset GL(\mathbb C^n)\), there are nonnegative integers \(\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). This is precisely Lentfer’s Theorem 1.2 / Corollary 1.3 in the \(S_n\) case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Taking ordinary Hilbert series replaces \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\) by \(f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the bosonic universal Hilbert coefficient is
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu .
\]
Therefore the same coefficients occur in the one-even, one-odd specialization:
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

The super Schur convention used here is
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\tag{2}
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only for \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\). This is the convention stated in Lentfer’s Equation (12). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). Hence
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{3}
\]
Also \(s_\varnothing(q/u)=1\). If \(\lambda\neq\varnothing\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so after removing any \((1^r)\) from \(\lambda'\), column \(2\) still contains at least two cells; hence no horizontal strip occurs and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

The degree-zero piece of the bosonic coinvariant algebra is spanned by \(1\), while every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Thus
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (3),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

We now compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove, over characteristic \(0\), that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k},
\tag{5}
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(u\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027)) Their Equation (2.5) gives the Schur expansion
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(n)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k){\rm des}(T)}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack n-k}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf z).
\tag{6}
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Taking dimensions in (6), and putting \(b=n-k\), gives for \(0\le b\le n-1\)
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(n)}
f^{\lambda(T)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, and with the usual convention \(i\in{\rm Des}(Q)\) iff \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\), one has
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Thus
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q .
\tag{7}
\]
For \(b\ge n\), (5) has no \(u^b\)-term, so \(H_b(q)=0\); the right side of (7) also vanishes because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1<b\). Hence (7) holds for every \(b\ge0\).

Now set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{8}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b+q^{-1}B_{b-1}\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (7). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity; it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\). This accounts for \(\delta_{b,0}\), proving (8).

Comparing (4) and (8) gives
\[
A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q)=B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\]
Since \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) yields \(A_b(q)=B_b(q)\) for all \(b\ge0\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

- [Standard theorem: row-insertion RSK descent preservation] VERIFIED — Standard Robinson–Schensted row insertion sends \(w\in S_n\) to \((P,Q)\), and \({\rm Des}(w)\) equals the descent set of the recording tableau \(Q\), matching the proof’s convention. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robinson%E2%80%93Schensted_correspondence))

([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_binomial_coefficient))

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
Add the following range clarification after defining the universal Hilbert coefficients:

For partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)>n\), set \(c_\lambda(n)=0\). Indeed, the cited supersymmetry/Hilbert expansion only has terms with \(\lambda\in P(k,0,n)\), hence \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\), for the bosonic algebra. By uniqueness of the stable Schur expansion, no coefficient of length \(>n\) can occur. Thus for hooks \((a,1^b)\) with \(b\ge n\), \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\). The proof’s recurrence comparison should then be read for \(0\le b\le n-1\); it proves \(A_b=B_b\) in that range. For \(b\ge n\), the left side is zero by \(\ell(a,1^b)=b+1>n\), and the right side is zero because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1<b+1\).
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

</FINAL_VERDICT>

<PROBLEM_SOLVED>
After the minor range clarification, the proof establishes the claimed hook interpretation over characteristic \(0\): \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
\]
</PROBLEM_SOLVED>
